summary: Gruber previews Apple's September 2009 'It's Only Rock and Roll' event, noting that Apple's secrecy machine has tightened since Steve Jobs's return. He predicts iPod Touch camera and spec bumps, a Nano camera addition, and a Classic capacity increase. Beyond hardware, he speculates about iTunes 9 potentially moving to a WebKit-based UI for cross-platform 64-bit compatibility, and theorizes that the rumored 'Cocktail' multimedia album format will be built on WebKit technologies. He also raises the question of whether Steve Jobs will appear on stage.
key_points: iPod Touch expected to get a camera and match iPhone 3GS specs, with a new 64GB tier at $399
The buttonless iPod Shuffle appears to be a sales dud, evidenced by Apple continuing to manufacture and sell the previous generation alongside it
iTunes faces a fundamental architectural problem: it needs to go 64-bit (requiring Cocoa), but there's no Cocoa runtime for Windows, suggesting a WebKit-based UI rewrite
The rumored 'Cocktail' multimedia format likely uses WebKit (HTML/CSS/JavaScript) to enable playback across Mac, Windows, iPhone, Apple TV, and a potential future tablet
Jobs's health and public appearance remain an elephant in the room — either he shows up gaunt or doesn't show up at all
money_quotes: The word is that Apple's 'secrecy machine' has been kicked back into high gear ever since you-know-who returned from you-know-what.
It's hard to see how the continuing demand for the old Shuffle isn't an indictment of the new Shuffle's design.

Common sense says it's never a good time for a low-level framework rewrite. But the writing is on the wall: the future is 64-bit, and the only path to 64-bit is Cocoa, so eventually, it has to happen.
What makes iTunes such an odd duck for Apple is that it's not just a Mac app — there are far more iTunes users on Windows.
I'm not saying iTunes will become a web browser — just that the UI itself will be a WebKit view. WebKit is cross-platform and built for the future.
It's going to be one or the other, folks.
So my guess is that Cocktail is going to be like a sort of next-generation Dashboard.
key_insight: Apple's cross-platform challenges with iTunes and new media formats point toward WebKit as the unifying technology that works across Mac, Windows, iPhone, and future devices.
